state.name: Rhode Island
state.agency: Rhode Island Department of Environmental Management, Division of Fish and Wildlife
state.peak_intrusion_season: March through May for spring denning, with a second wave from October into early winter as animals seek warm shelter
state.climate_factor: Rhode Island's coastal New England climate brings cold winters that drive rodents and bats indoors aggressively, while mild humid summers fuel rapid wildlife reproduction in suburban and urban areas
state.permit_note: Rhode Island requires a Nuisance Wildlife Control permit for commercial trappers, and bats and migratory birds carry additional state and federal handling restrictions

Providence County.region: northern Rhode Island, the most populous and urbanized part of the state
Providence County.habitat_type: dense urban Providence and surrounding mill cities, with wooded suburbs and small river valleys to the north and west
Providence County.regional_wildlife: Norway rat populations are dense in the older neighborhoods of Providence, Pawtucket, and Woonsocket, raccoons routinely raid attics and dumpsters across the urban core, and bat colonies in older multifamily homes throughout the city are a recurring summer call source
Providence County.absent_or_rare: coyotes are heavily established throughout Rhode Island and white-tailed deer move through the wooded northern townships, but both species fall under RI DEM Fish and Wildlife management rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope

Kent County.region: central Rhode Island west of Narragansett Bay
Kent County.habitat_type: suburban neighborhoods along the bay shore transitioning to wooded interior in western Coventry and West Greenwich
Kent County.regional_wildlife: Warwick and the Greenwich Bay shoreline communities see steady raccoon and skunk pressure in attics and under decks, moles damage manicured lawns throughout the suburban Cranston-edge and East Greenwich neighborhoods, and the wooded interior of Coventry and West Greenwich generates occasional fisher and porcupine outbuilding damage
Kent County.absent_or_rare: coyotes are well-established across every Kent County town and white-tailed deer move through the wooded western towns, but both species are managed by RI DEM Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Washington County.region: southern Rhode Island along the Atlantic coast and Narragansett Bay's western shore
Washington County.habitat_type: coastal salt ponds, pine and oak forest, and farmland — the most rural part of the state
Washington County.regional_wildlife: coastal vacation homes near the salt ponds and around Burlingame State Park generate heavy bat colony activity, particularly in structures that sit empty through the off-season, fishers cause regular chicken-coop and outbuilding damage in the inland pine and oak forest, beavers flood streams and culverts along the rural waterways, and moles damage lawns at coastal residential properties throughout South Kingstown, North Kingstown, and Westerly
Washington County.absent_or_rare: coyotes are common throughout the rural southern Rhode Island countryside and white-tailed deer are abundant in the inland forest, but both species fall under RI DEM Fish and Wildlife jurisdiction rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope

Newport County.region: Aquidneck Island, Conanicut Island, and several smaller islands and a small mainland town
Newport County.habitat_type: coastal islands with historic estates, suburban neighborhoods, and undeveloped shoreline
Newport County.regional_wildlife: Newport's historic mansions and older homes throughout Aquidneck Island host large bat colonies that require summer maternity-season exclusion work, raccoons are persistent at waterfront properties across Newport, Middletown, and Portsmouth, Norway rats are concentrated in the downtown Newport waterfront commercial blocks, and pigeon nuisance issues are widespread on the historic-building stock
Newport County.absent_or_rare: coyotes have established on Aquidneck Island in recent years and white-tailed deer move through the more wooded mainland edge, but both species are managed by RI DEM Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors; the islands' water barrier limits other forest mammals common on the mainland

Bristol County.region: a small peninsula between the Providence River and Mount Hope Bay, the smallest county in the smallest state
Bristol County.habitat_type: a fully developed coastal peninsula with historic neighborhoods, small wooded parcels, and tidal shoreline
Bristol County.regional_wildlife: raccoons, striped skunks, and opossums are persistent residential nuisance species across the developed peninsula, bat colonies in older homes throughout Bristol and Warren are a recurring summer issue, Norway rats are concentrated in the Mount Hope Bay waterfront blocks and Bristol's downtown commercial corridor, and moles damage manicured lawns throughout the residential neighborhoods of Barrington, Warren, and Bristol
Bristol County.absent_or_rare: coyotes are well-established across the peninsula and white-tailed deer occasionally move through the more wooded edges, but both species fall under RI DEM Fish and Wildlife management rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope
